div.div: Effect: Beads threaded on two intertwined strings appear inextricably trapped — yet the performer removes them instantly. Method: before the audience sees the setup, cross the two strings at their midpoint, then thread the beads over the crossed section. The beads appear to be strung straight through, but they're sitting on crossed strings. When spectators hold the four ends and pull, the beads seem locked. Under cover of your hand 'working the magic,' uncross the center — the beads slip free as the strings separate.

String-and-bead tricks formed one of the largest categories of Victorian parlor magic because they required no special equipment — any household had strings and beads. The mathematical secret (crossing strings) was considered elegant by the era's many amateur mathematicians.

Thread beads onto two strings twisted together and tied at their ends. Spectators pull both ends and the beads seem inextricably locked — yet the magician removes them easily. The secret: strings are secretly crossed at the center, and the center bead is passed over the juncture; pulling loops free releases all beads under cover of hand.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
